text: Last night, paramedics rushed in with a man injured in a car crash. He had broken his leg before arrival, and his face was covered in blood. Nurses quickly took him for X-rays while Dr. Maha prepared to stitch a deep cut on his forehead. "Teamwork saved him," said the emergency room "ER" director proudly. Outside, it was raining cats and dogs as Dr. Basem ran into the hospital, soaked to the skin. Inside, the emergency room was in full chaos—patients groaning, nurses hurrying, and machines beeping non-stop. In one corner, a teenager with a broken arm moaned quietly. A construction worker, pale as a ghost, clutched his bleeding hand, trying not to cry out. Nurse Noura, calm under pressure, gave clear and confident instructions. "Prepare the sterile equipment and state its condition before use", she ordered. Suddenly, the ER doors flew open. A panicked mother rushed in, holding her toddler. "He's not responding!", she shouted. The child had swallowed a coin. Without hesitation, the team jumped into action. Using a flexible scope, they carefully removed the coin. Moments later, the child opened his eyes. The mother wept with relief and gratitude. Around her, staff members smiled—some hiding their own tears. As the sun began to rise, calm slowly returned. Dr. Basem sat down, exhausted. Around him, the team cleaned up and checked medical charts. Another night filled with fear, urgency, and life-saving teamwork had come to an end.

Hello everyone! I want to share something that happened to me recently. About two weeks ago, I started to feel a little tired. I also had a small cough. I didn't think it was very serious at first. But then, the symptoms got much worse. My throat was very sore, and my nose was running all the time. I also had a bad headache and felt very tired. It was difficult to sleep at night because of the cough. I felt really miserable and uncomfortable. After a few days, I decided I needed to see a doctor. So, I called my clinic and made an appointment. When I arrived, the waiting room was full of other people who also looked sick. After a short wait, the doctor called my name. He asked me many questions about my symptoms and how long I had felt bad. Then, he checked my throat, listened to my chest with a stethoscope, and took my temperature. He said I had a severe viral cold. The doctor gave me a prescription for some medicine to help with the symptoms. He also told me to rest a lot and drink many warm liquids like tea. I went home, took the medicine, and tried to sleep as much as possible. Slowly, after about three or four days, I started to feel much better. Now, I am healthy again, and I feel very happy about it! This experience reminded me how important our health is. It's also good to know that doctors are there to help us when we need them. Have you ever had a very bad cold? How do you usually recover? Let me know in the comments!

A blog post is an article or a piece of writing published on a blog, which is an online journal or website. Blog posts are usually informal or semi-formal, like talking to a friend but in a more organized way. They are engaging and aim to catch the reader's attention. Blog posts are about a specific topic and share information, opinions, or personal experiences. They are not too long and are easy to read online.

Steps to write a blog post - A good blog post usually has these parts: 1. Catchy Title or Headline: Purpose is to make people want to click and read. Examples: 5 Amazing Places to Visit in Summer, My First Time Trying Sushi and Why I Loved It, Is Learning English Hard? My Opinion. 2. Introduction: Purpose is to introduce your topic and hook the reader. Example: Have you ever dreamed of traveling the world? For me, visiting new countries has always been a passion. Today, I want to share my recent trip to Japan and some amazing discoveries I made there. 3. Main Body: Purpose is to develop your ideas, provide details, and explain your points. Example: Paragraph 1 about Food: One of the best parts of my trip was the food. I tried sushi, ramen, and tempura. The ramen was especially delicious because... Paragraph 2 about Culture: Apart from food, I was fascinated by the traditional culture. I visited several temples and saw a tea ceremony. 4. Conclusion: Purpose is to summarize your main points and give a final thought. Example: Overall, my trip to Japan was an unforgettable experience. I highly recommend visiting this amazing country if you get the chance. Have you ever been to Japan? What was your favorite part? Tips on language and style: Use simple, clear language. Keep paragraphs short with 2-5 sentences. Write in a friendly, personal tone. Use the first person I or we if it's a personal or narrative post. Add expressive language such as idioms or emotions when appropriate.

Blog post terminology: Audience means the readers the post is intended for, such as teenagers, students, or general public. Blog is a regularly updated website or journal that contains individual entries called blog posts. Blog Post is a single article or entry published on a blog. Call to Action or CTA is a sentence encouraging the reader to take an action such as comment, share, or subscribe. Catchy Title is a headline designed to attract clicks and grab the reader's attention. Hook is an engaging opening that grabs the reader's attention, such as a question, quote, fact, or anecdote. Subheading is a smaller heading used to divide sections of a blog post for easier reading. Title is a catchy and clear headline that summarizes the blog post's content. Tone is the style or attitude of the writer, such as formal, informal, humorous, or serious. Topic is the subject or theme of the blog post. Transition Words are words like first, next, however, finally that help connect ideas smoothly.

Model 1 - Introduction for blog post titled 5 Reasons Why to Visit Aswan in Winter: Have you ever dreamed of escaping the cold while still enjoying the magic of winter? Aswan, one of Egypt's most charming cities, offers the perfect warm-weather getaway during the cooler months. With its golden sunshine, peaceful Nile views, and rich cultural heritage, Aswan becomes a magical destination between November and February. Whether you're a history lover, an adventure seeker, or simply someone looking to relax, Aswan in winter has something just for you. In this post, I'll share five great reasons why this southern gem should be at the top of your winter travel list.

Model 2 - Body Paragraph for blog post titled 5 Reasons Why to Visit Aswan in Winter: Unlike other tourist hotspots, Aswan stays calm and uncrowded during winter. This means you can explore temples like Philae or stroll along the Nile without being rushed or surrounded by large groups. The peaceful atmosphere allows you to truly connect with the history and beauty around you. Local markets are less busy, so you can chat with friendly vendors and take your time picking souvenirs. Even the felucca rides feel more personal and relaxed. It's the perfect setting for travelers who enjoy slow, meaningful experiences.

Model 3 - Conclusion for blog post titled 5 Reasons Why to Visit Aswan in Winter: To conclude, Aswan in winter offers more than just sunshine - it gives you calm, cultural, and unforgettable memories. From its mild weather to peaceful sights, it's the perfect winter escape. You can enjoy local life, explore history, and relax by the Nile. Whether you're an adventurer, a culture lover, or just someone who needs a break. To sum up, Aswan welcomes you warmly. So why wait? Pack your bags and discover the magic of Aswan this winter!

Summary of A Day in the Life of a Hospital Nurse: 1. The text describes a typical day for Nurse Sarah, which involves a 12-hour shift. 2. Her duties include checking patient charts, administering medication, and monitoring patients. 3. She faces emergencies, such as a car accident victim being rushed in. 4. Despite the chaos and exhaustion, Sarah finds her job fulfilling and rewarding. 5. The text highlights that nursing is a roller coaster of emotions, with both heartbreaking and rewarding moments, but she wouldn't trade it for anything. Summary of Emergency call: 1. A caller reports to an emergency operator that his neighbour has collapsed. 2. The neighbour suddenly grabbed his chest and fell while walking downstairs. 3. The operator confirms the patient is not breathing and that CPR has been started by the man's wife. 4. The caller mentions the patient had complained about chest pain earlier, but his medication had not been taken. 5. The operator tells the caller that help is on the way and tries to determine if the patient regained consciousness. Summary of Tired Hands, Caring Hearts: 1. Doctors and nurses work long, hard hours, often missing meals or rest. 2. They must make quick, life-saving decisions and perform operations, which can be very tiring. 3. The work is emotionally demanding as they see people in pain and sometimes lose patients. 4. Despite the challenges, their work shows true dedication and compassion, bringing hope and healing to others. Summary of Night in the ER: 1. The text describes a chaotic night in an emergency room, starting with paramedics rushing in with a man injured in a car crash. 2. The ER is filled with chaos: groaning patients, hurrying nurses, and non-stop machines. 3. Nurse Noura calmly handles the pressure, giving clear instructions and preparing the sterile equipment. 4. A panicked mother arrives with a child who has swallowed a coin, and the team jumps into action, successfully removing the coin with a flexible scope. 5. The night ends with a sense of relief and accomplishment, showing that another night of urgent, life-saving teamwork has come to an end. Summary of My Unwelcome Visitor A Recent Cold Experience: 1. The writer describes a personal experience of getting sick with what started as a small cough. 2. The symptoms worsened to a sore throat, runny nose, headache, and exhaustion, making it difficult to sleep. 3. After a few days, the writer decided to see a doctor. 4. The doctor diagnosed a severe viral cold, checked the throat and listened to my chest with a stethoscope, and gave a prescription for medicine. 5. After about four days of rest and medication, the writer felt healthy again and gained a new appreciation for the importance of doctors.

Reading: Read short texts, articles, and conversations about machines, technology, and their historical context in Egypt. Identify key details, and understand idioms.
Language: Use and apply reported speech for statements, commands, and requests.
Listening: Understand and extract key information from an audio conversation, specifically a technical support call.
Writing: Write an argumentative essay with a clear opinion, supporting reasons, and a counter argument.
Speaking: Talk about machines and their impact, present an invented machine. Give and follow instructions.

metadata.lesson_title: Machines in Egypt: Past and Present / Technical Support Call

text: Egypt, a land rich in ancient wonders, has a long and fascinating history with machines. From the very basic tools of the early dynasties to the sophisticated devices of today, machines have played an important role in shaping the nation. In ancient times, Egyptians relied heavily on simple machines like levers and ramps to build their monumental pyramids and temples. Historians believe that these early innovations laid the groundwork for future engineering marvels. One expert noted, "The Egyptians were masters of mechanics long before the modern era." During the Industrial Revolution, Egypt began importing advanced machinery from Europe. Cotton gins and steam engines revolutionized agriculture and transportation. However, not all changes were smooth; some traditional crafts disappeared as machines took over. Today, Egypt continues to adopt modern technology, from computerized factories to renewable energy systems. While some fear that machines may replace human labor, others argue they enhance productivity. As one engineer remarked, "Machines are tools, not replacements for human skills." Now, Egypt is also working with advanced technology like artificial intelligence and robots. Government initiatives and private-sector partnerships are driving forward projects in automation and smart infrastructure. Yet, even as the country charges full steam ahead into the digital age, it remains deeply connected to its past—museums now use 3D scanning to preserve ancient artifacts, blending old and new.

metadata.lesson_title: Machines in Egypt: Past and Present

text:
Technician: TechHelp Support, this is Rami speaking. How can I assist you today?
Customer: Hi Rami, this is Rania from Apex Marketing. Our printer isn't working properly, and we have an important presentation tomorrow.
Technician: I understand your urgency, Rania. Let me help you troubleshoot this. First, please check if the printer is properly connected to your computer.
Customer: Okay, yes, all the cables are secure.
Technician: Great. Now, I need you to open the printer settings on your computer. Can you see the printer listed there?
Customer: Yes, but it shows as 'offline'.
Technician: Perfect, that's the issue. Please right-click on the printer icon and select 'Use Printer Online'. Then, try printing a test page.
Customer: Wow, it's working now! Thank you so much, Rami.
Technician: You're welcome! Now, to prevent this from happening again, I recommend restarting both your computer and printer once a week. Also, please don't turn off the printer while it's processing jobs.
Customer: Got it. Should I do anything else?
Technician: I suggest keeping the printer drivers updated. Our website has the latest versions. Is there anything else I can help you with today?
Customer: No, that's perfect. Thanks again!

Machines play an important role in our daily lives. They save time, reduce human effort, and make many tasks easier. At the hospital, for example, doctors use machines to monitor patients' hearts and give accurate diagnoses. Last week, a doctor said that machines helped him detect illnesses more quickly. Nurses also mentioned that machines made their work smoother and reduced mistakes. In factories, machines are always running. They cut, assemble, and pack products at a speed no human can match. A factory manager explained that machines could work all day without getting tired. However, he also warned that humans should check the machines regularly. At school, students learn that machines are both helpful and dangerous. A teacher told them that machines could save lives but might also cause accidents if not used carefully. Another student added that machines were changing the future of jobs. Although machines are powerful, they still need human control. People say that machines cannot replace creativity or compassion.

Reported Speech Statement - Formation: Reporter plus reporting verb plus that (optional) plus reported speech content. Examples: Sama said that her father worked in a secondary school. Mr Omar said that he was traveling to Cairo the next day. The reporting verb comes after the reporter. There are two types of reporting verbs: First type - reporting verbs that must be followed by the addressee plus object, such as: told, reminded, warned, persuaded, convinced. The addressee is a noun or object pronoun (me, him, her, it, you, us, them). Examples: Mohammed told Omar that he wanted to go swimming. Nada reminded me that we had to get up early the day after. Second type - reporting verbs that are not followed by the addressee, such as: said, admitted, shouted, remarked, whispered, cried, complained, explained, replied, answered, reported. Examples: Yara said that her mother was very busy. The worker complained that the weather had been too hot the day before. Do not use quotation marks in reported speech. Direct: "I want to buy a new tablet", said Rodayna. Reported: Rodayna said that she wanted to buy a new tablet. You can use that or omit it with the verb say, but it is preferable to use it after other reporting verbs. Examples: Leen said that she could help me. Bassem explained that he had an important meeting.

Pronoun changes in reported speech according to the speaker and addressee: Direct I becomes Reported he, she, you, I. Direct we becomes Reported they, we. Direct me becomes Reported him, her, you, me. Direct us becomes Reported them, us. Direct my becomes Reported his, her, your, my. Direct our becomes Reported their, our. Direct mine becomes Reported his, hers, yours, mine. Direct ours becomes Reported theirs, ours. Direct you as verb becomes Reported he, she, they, we, you. Direct you as object becomes Reported me, him, her, them, us. Direct your becomes Reported my, his, her, their, our. Direct yours becomes Reported mine, his, hers, theirs, ours, yours. Some time and place adverbs differ in reported speech from direct speech: Direct: "These chairs will be moved here tomorrow", said Nada. Reported: Nada said that those chairs would be moved there the following day. Direct: "I haven't finished this report yet", explained Youssef. Reported: Youssef explained that he hadn't finished that report by then.

Important adverb transformations in reported speech: Direct now becomes Reported then or at that time. Direct this becomes Reported that. Direct these becomes Reported those. Direct here becomes Reported there. Direct ago becomes Reported before or earlier. Direct last week becomes Reported the week before or the previous week. Direct yesterday becomes Reported the day before or the previous day. Direct tomorrow becomes Reported the day after or the following day. Direct tonight becomes Reported that night. Direct today becomes Reported that day. Direct yet becomes Reported by then. Direct the day before yesterday becomes Reported two days before. Direct the day after tomorrow becomes Reported in two days' time. Direct next week becomes Reported the week after or the following week.

Tense changes in reported speech - tenses shift backwards, meaning each tense converts to its corresponding past tense: Direct: "My wife likes green salad", said Magdi (present simple). Reported: Magdi said that his wife liked green salad (past simple). Direct: My cousin said, "I am watching an action film" (present continuous). Reported: My cousin said that she was watching an action film (past continuous). Summary of tense transformations: Direct play/plays (present simple) becomes Reported played (past simple). Direct played (past simple) becomes Reported had played or played (past perfect simple). Direct am/is/are (auxiliary verb) becomes Reported was/were. Direct was/were (main verb) becomes Reported was/were or had been. Direct have/has (main verb) becomes Reported had. Direct had (main verb) becomes Reported had or had had. Direct can/will/may become Reported could/would/might. Direct shall becomes Reported should/would. Direct must becomes Reported must or had to or would have to. Direct needn't becomes Reported didn't have to or wouldn't have to. Past simple can remain unchanged provided it does not lead to ambiguity in meaning: He said that he arrived or had arrived on Friday.

Transformations of the modal verb must in reported speech: First case - must remains unchanged when expressing laws: Direct: "Drivers must hold a driving licence", said Omar. Reported: Omar said that drivers must hold a driving licence. Second case - must converts to had to if indicating present necessity: Direct: "You must call the manager now, Ali", said Aya. Reported: Aya told Ali that he had to call the manager then. Third case - must converts to would have to if indicating future necessity: Direct: "I must return home next month", said Tom. Reported: Tom said that he would have to return home the following month. Important notes: There is no transformation for past perfect and past perfect continuous tenses. Direct: "I had returned home by midday", said Ahmed. Reported: Ahmed said that he had returned home by midday. There is no transformation for the past form of modal verbs: would, could, might, should, would rather, had better. Direct: "You should study hard, Samar", said mum. Reported: Mum told Samar that she should study hard. Past simple remains unchanged after the following expressions: if, would rather, I wish, it is time. Direct: "It is time you went home, Tarek", said Uncle Yahia. Reported: Uncle Yahia told Tarek that it was time he went home. Used to indicating habit remains unchanged. Direct: She said, "I used to get up late". Reported: She said that she used to get up late.

Important notes on when tenses do not change in reported speech: Tense does not change if the sentence expresses an unchangeable fact. Direct: "Water boils at 100°C" she said. Reported: She said that water boils at 100°C. Tenses may or may not change with sentences indicating relative facts that may change. Direct: "Lake Nasser is the largest man-made lake," he said. Reported: He said that Lake Nasser is or was the largest man-made lake. Time and place adverbs and tenses do not change if the time period or location has not changed. Example: Ten minutes ago, Aya said to me, "I will call you next Friday". Ten minutes ago, Aya told me that she will call me next Friday. Tenses do not change if the sentence was said a short time ago, indicated by expressions such as: said a moment ago, a minute ago, a short time ago, just now, etc. Direct: "We will go out soon", Areej said a moment ago. Reported: A moment ago, Areej said that they will go out soon. Tenses do not change if the reporting verb is in the present tense. Direct: Walid says to me, "My parents work hard." Reported: Walid tells me that his parents work hard. Agreement or affirmation phrases such as Yes, Sure, Of course are deleted and a reporting verb indicating agreement or affirmation is used such as: agreed, approved, answered in affirmative, etc. Direct: "Yes, I have received your email," said Sama. Reported: Sama agreed that she had received my email. Refusal or disagreement phrases such as No, Sorry are deleted and a reporting verb indicating refusal or disagreement is used such as: denied, disapproved, answered in negative, etc. Direct: "No, I didn't see Ali today," said Ahmed. Reported: Ahmed denied that he had seen Ali that day.

Reported Commands and Requests - Affirmative commands (do) in direct speech begin with the verb in infinitive form. Example: "Open the door, Leen", said mum. Negative commands (don't do) in direct speech begin with Don't or Never plus infinitive. Example: "Don't/Never eat too much, Amr", said father. Commands and prohibitions in reported speech consist of: reporter plus reporting verb plus addressee plus to or not to plus infinitive. Examples: Mom asked Leen to open the door. Father warned Amr not to eat too much. An appropriate reporting verb is used followed by the addressee (noun or object pronoun): told, asked, ordered, commanded, reminded, warned, advised, encouraged, instructed, invited, wanted, helped, threatened, expected, taught. Examples: I told Sama to help her mother. Grandpa advised me to work hard at school. Do not use quotation marks in commands or prohibitions in reported speech. Direct: "Never play with matches", said my mother. Reported: My mother warned me not to play with matches. Use to plus infinitive meaning (to) in affirmative. Example: I told him to do more exercise. Use not to plus infinitive meaning (not to) in negative. Example: The officer ordered the soldiers not to leave their places.

Pronouns change in reported commands according to the speaker and addressee: Direct: Amira said, "Lend me your camera, Ali". Reported: Amira asked Ali to lend her his camera. Some time and place adverbs change: Direct: "Bring this book with you tomorrow", said Miss Mona. Reported: Miss Mona asked me to bring that book with me the following day. Important notes on transforming advice sentences: Direct: "You should study hard, Malak", said Mr Hassan. Reported: Mr Hassan advised or told Malak to study hard. Direct: "If I were you, I'd get up early", said Aya. Reported: Aya advised me to get up early. Important notes on transforming request sentences: Direct: "Can you lend me some money, Omar?", said Mustafa. Reported: Mustafa asked or wanted Omar to lend him some money. When transforming suggestion sentences to reported speech, you can use the verbs suggest or recommend followed by verb-ing. Direct: "What about going to the cinema?", said Doaa. Reported: Doaa suggested or recommended going to the cinema. If there is a clause after the following verbs and expressions: suggest, recommend, ask, insist, advise, it is or was vital, essential, crucial, necessary, important, the verb is infinitive or should plus infinitive. Examples: Ahmed suggested or advised that Sama take a rest equals Ahmed suggested or advised that Sama should take a rest. It is important that Omar see a doctor. It is essential that he be fit. It is important that Omar should see a doctor. It is essential that he should be fit. Come here transforms to go there in reported speech. Direct: "Come here, Emma," Said mum. Reported: Mum asked Emma to go there.

metadata.lesson_title: Our Machine-Driven World / For or Against?

text: Nowadays, machines are a big part of our daily routines. From the moment we wake up, they influence what we do. The alarm clock rings, the kettle boils, and the bus or metro takes us to school or work - all powered by machines. It's hard to imagine life without them. At home, we depend on household appliances like washing machines, microwaves, and vacuum cleaners. These devices save time and make chores easier. A recent study reported that most people use over five machines every single day. Even our free time is shaped by technology - smartphones, laptops, and smart TVs that keep us entertained and connected with friends and family. In the workplace, machines help us work faster and more accurately. Office workers use computers to write emails, prepare documents, and join online meetings. In factories, robots build cars, pack products, and carry heavy items. One factory manager said, "Our production has doubled since we started using automated systems." Our health is also monitored by machines. Smartwatches track our heart rate, steps, and sleep quality. Doctors use advanced scanners and machines to detect illnesses early and plan treatments. However, some people worry that we rely too much on machines. They fear that if technology fails, our lives could come to a standstill. As one teacher explained, "Students struggle to write without spellcheck or grammar tools." While machines bring comfort, speed, and efficiency, it's important to remember that we should control them not the other way around. Life with machines is easier, but we must stay in charge of our choices and skills. After all, technology should serve us, not rule us.

Smartphones can be valuable learning tools in the classroom; however, their use also presents challenges. Firstly, students can quickly access information, for example, they can look up a word they don't know or find an image related to a lesson. This instant access to information can make learning more engaging and interactive. On the other hand, smartphones can be a major distraction because students may be tempted to use them for social media or games instead of focusing on the lesson. This can disrupt not only their own learning but also that of their classmates. In conclusion, while phones offer great educational potential, a balance must be found to maximize the benefits while minimizing the drawbacks.

An argumentative essay is a type of writing where you give your claim on a topic and support it with reasons and evidence. You also mention the opposite claim and explain why you think your claim is stronger.

Structure and Key Components of an Argumentative Essay: 1. Introduction: Introduce the topic, give background, and state your claim (thesis). 2. Body Paragraphs: a. Supporting Paragraphs: Present at least two reasons for your claim with examples or evidence. b. Opposing View: Mention the opposite side and say why you disagree (refutation). 3. Conclusion: Restate your claim and summarize your main points. Useful Expressions: Stating your claim: In my opinion..., I believe that..., I think it is clear that... Supporting your claim: Firstly..., For example..., Another reason is... Introducing the other side: Some people say that..., On the other hand..., It is argued that... Refuting the other side: However, I disagree because..., I believe this is not strong enough because..., This may be true, but... Concluding: In conclusion..., That's why I believe..., To sum up..., In brief...

Argumentative essay terminology: Essay: A piece of writing about one topic. It has an introduction, body, and conclusion. Argumentative essay: An essay that gives an opinion and supports it with reasons. Opinion: What you believe or think. Reason: Why you think your opinion is right. Evidence: A fact or example that supports your reason. Introduction: The beginning of the essay that tells the topic and your opinion. Body paragraph: A paragraph in the middle that gives reasons and examples. Supporting paragraph: A body paragraph that explains your opinion. Opposing view: The other side of the argument. Refutation: Saying why the opposing view is weak or wrong. Conclusion: The last part that restates your opinion and summarizes the main points. Transition word: A word that connects ideas (e.g., however, firstly). Topic sentence: The main idea of a paragraph. Thesis statement: A sentence in the introduction that shows your main opinion. Hook: An interesting sentence at the start to catch the reader's attention. Clarify: To make your ideas easy to understand.

Introduction Model for argumentative essay on The Power of Machines: In today's world, machines play an essential role in almost every part of our lives. From smart devices to industrial robots, their influence continues to grow. Many people believe machines make life easier and more productive. Others worry that machines are taking over human jobs and thinking. However, I believe the power of machines is mostly positive. With the right use, machines can improve our lives and solve big problems.

Body Paragraph Model for argumentative essay on The Power of Machines: Firstly, machines help us complete tasks faster and with fewer mistakes. In factories, for example, machines produce goods much faster than humans can. They also reduce the risk of injury in dangerous jobs like mining or construction. Moreover, smart machines like AI can help in education, medicine, and transport. Some argue that machines replace people, but they also create new jobs in tech. Therefore, the benefits of machines far outweigh the disadvantages.

Conclusion Model for argumentative essay on The Power of Machines: To sum up, machines have become powerful tools in our modern world. They help us work smarter, live more comfortably, and even stay healthier. Although there are concerns about job loss, new opportunities also appear. The key is learning how to use machines wisely and responsibly. In brief, we should focus on guiding machine power, not fearing it. That's why I believe machines are a great support to human progress.

Summary of Egypt's History with Machines: Ancient Egyptians were masters of mechanics, using simple machines like levers and ramps to build monumental pyramids and temples. The Industrial Revolution in the 19th century introduced advanced technology like cotton gins and steam engines to Egypt, revolutionizing agriculture and transportation. Today, Egypt continues to adopt modern technology, from computerized factories to advanced AI and robotics. While embracing the future, Egypt also preserves its past, using technology like 3D scanning to protect ancient artifacts. Summary of Technical Support Call: A customer named Rania calls technical support because her printer is not working and appears offline. The technician calmly guides her through troubleshooting steps over the phone. He instructs her to open the printer settings, right-click on the printer icon, and select Use Printer Online. The solution works, and the technician advises Rania to keep her printer drivers updated and avoid turning it off while it's processing jobs. Summary of The Role of Machines: Machines play a vital role in daily life, saving time and reducing human effort. In hospitals, they provide accurate diagnoses and monitor patients' hearts. In factories, machines run continuously to assemble and pack products at a speed humans cannot match. Although machines are powerful, they still need human control, and they cannot replace human creativity or compassion. Summary of Machines in Our Daily Routines: Modern life is heavily dependent on machines, from alarm clocks and kettles in the morning to transportation. Household appliances like washing machines and vacuum cleaners make chores easier and save time. In the workplace, machines enhance productivity and accuracy. Smart devices like phones and watches keep us connected and monitor our health. While technology offers comfort and efficiency, there's a risk of over-reliance, as a system failure can cause a standstill. Summary of Smartphones in the Classroom: Smartphones can be valuable educational tools, giving students instant access to information. However, they can also be a major distraction, tempting students with social media and games. This distraction can disrupt not only the user's focus but also that of their classmates. The key is to find a balance between using the benefits of smartphones and minimizing their potential drawbacks to create a productive learning environment.

Reading: Read articles and texts on climate change, climate justice, and global environmental issues. Extract key details, define vocabulary from context. Understand cause-and-effect relationships.
Language: Use modal verbs for logical deduction in the present and past. Express blame or regret.
Listening: Extract key information from an audio conversation, specifically a radio interview with a meteorologist.
Writing: Write a persuasive essay on the topic of climate change, using specific vocabulary and persuasive techniques.
Speaking: Participate in group discussions and debates about climate change, its effects, and the role of individuals and youth activists.

metadata.lesson_title: Climate Justice and Global Activism / An Interview with a Meteorologist

text:

text:
Weather Expressions: scorching hot = very hot شديد الحرارة, boiling = extremely hot حار للغاية, baking = very sunny and hot محرق / مشمش جدًا, freezing cold = very cold بارد جدا, chilly = a little cold فاتر / بارد, pouring with rain = raining heavily تمطر بشدة, humid = hot and wet رطب, stormy = with strong winds and rain عاصف, foggy = with thick mist in the air ضبابي, overcast = cloudy and grey ملبد بالغيوم.

Climate vs. Weather: climate (n): المناخ (حالة الجو على المدى الطويل في منطقة معينة), weather (n): الطقس (حالة الجو اليومية). Note: Weather is short-term and changes daily. Climate is long-term and stable over time.

Drought vs. Draught: drought (n): جفاف, draught / draft (n): نسمة هواء باردة / تيار هواء. Note: Drought relates to climate and water. Draught is a cold breeze or a sip (British English).

Important Idioms: bear the brunt: to suffer the worst part of something bad (يتحمل الجزء الأكبر من الضرر), take the heat: to accept criticism or blame (يقبل النقد أو اللوم), shoulder the burden: to take responsibility for something difficult or unpleasant (يتحمل مسئولية صعبة), face the music: to accept the unpleasant results of your actions (يواجه العواقب أو النتائج السيئة).

Equality, Equity, Fairness, Justice: equality (n): الجميع يحصل على نفس الشيء أو الفرصة, equity (n): يحصل كل شخص على ما يحتاجه فعلاً ليصل لنفس النتيجة العادلة, fairness (n): معاملة الناس بطريقة محايدة دون تحيز, justice (n): نظام عادل لتحقيق العدل تحت مظلة المبادئ والقوانين.

Forecast vs. Predict: forecast (v): يتنبأ بناء على معلومات (أكثر تقنية مثل الطقس أو الاقتصاد), predict (v): يتوقع (بشكل عام أو شخصي بناء على منطق أو اعتقاد).

metadata.lesson_title: Climate Justice and Global Activism / An Interview with a Meteorologist

text: Climate change must be seen not only as an environmental crisis but also as a profound issue of global justice. While industrialized nations are largely responsible for the majority of greenhouse gas emissions, it is often low-income and marginalized communities - particularly in the Global South - that bear the brunt of its consequences. These populations might face rising sea levels, long droughts, food shortage, and extreme weather events that threaten their health and safety. Climate justice emphasizes that we cannot ignore these effects. It calls for fair policies, shared decisions, and cooperation to fix the unequal burdens of climate change. People who cause the least damage must not suffer the most.

Youth around the world are actively working to stop climate change, showing great leadership and creativity. One initiative is the "Youth Climate Lab" which empowers young innovators to develop solutions for climate challenges, such as creating sustainable energy projects or designing waste reduction programs. Their efforts have resulted in tangible changes, like the implementation of community solar projects and successful recycling campaigns in various regions. At its core, climate justice is about equity, responsibility, and giving a voice to those who are often unheard in global debates. Without justice, there can be no true solution to the climate crisis.

Presenter: Good evening, listeners. With the recent scorching heat across Egypt, one topic everyone's talking about is - yes, you guessed it - the weather. Joining us today in the studio is meteorologist Tamer Mansour. Earlier, we asked you to share your questions, and now Tamer is here to answer a few. Welcome, Tamer!
Tamer: Thanks! Great to be here.
Presenter: First question from our audience: What's the difference between a meteorologist and a weather presenter?
Tamer: Well, a meteorologist collects and analyzes weather data, while a presenter simply delivers that information, usually on TV or radio. Some presenters are trained meteorologists, but not many.
Presenter: And how far ahead can you predict the weather?
Tamer: Usually five to seven days. But some weather types are easier to predict than others.
Presenter: Have you noticed any changes in Egypt's weather over the last decade?
Tamer: Not dramatically - but this year's heatwave is unusual. Extreme weather might be becoming more frequent globally, and it certainly seems to be getting hotter.
Presenter: Are you optimistic about climate change?
Tamer: Honestly, I'm a bit pessimistic. In Egypt, we'll likely face more extreme heatwaves in the future.
Presenter: Thanks, Tamer. Fascinating insights - and hopefully cooler weather soon!

Coastal cities such as Alexandria face great risks from climate change. As global temperatures rise, glaciers and ice sheets melt, and sea levels increase. Scientists emphasize that even a small rise in sea level can cause serious flooding in low-lying areas that can stop business for days. This must be one of the greatest threats to homes, schools, and hospitals. Experts warn that the damage might be worse than many people think, and floods could be happening more often in the near future.

In Alexandria, many people live close to the sea. They notice that the waves are stronger and sometimes reach the streets. Families worry about their homes and shop owners worry about their shops, but they also take precautions to stay safe. Fishermen explain that the sea is changing. They say the waves must have been caused by global warming, since storms can't have become stronger for no reason. Still, people remain hopeful and try to prepare in advance.

The state is also working to reduce the dangers. It builds sea walls to protect neighborhoods and invests in renewable energy to reduce pollution. Authorities run awareness campaigns to teach citizens how to act during storms. Officials admit that without these measures, the losses might have been far greater. With these efforts, the risks may be reduced, and Alexandria can remain safe for future generations. Many experts also argue that we shouldn't have ignored the problem of climate change. Citizens should have taken more responsibility for reducing pollution in their daily lives.

Modal verbs used for deduction: must (لابد): تستخدم للتعبير عن استنتاج مثبت مبنى على دليل قوي (شعور بالتأكد). Example: He has eaten nothing all day. He must be hungry. can't (لا يمكن): تستخدم للتعبير عن استنتاج منفي مبنى على دليل قوي (شعور بالتأكد). Example: He has eaten nothing all day. He can't be full. might/may/could (ربما / من المحتمل / قد): تستخدم للتعبير عن استنتاج مبنى على دليل ضعيف (عدم تأكد). Example: I don't know why Sama isn't at the party. She might be ill. She may have a problem. She could be in a bad mood.

Important notes on modal verbs of deduction: Deduction is based on evidence, not absolute knowledge. The opposite of "must" in deduction is "can't", not "mustn't". "must" and "can't/couldn't" express high certainty (I feel sure, certainly, surely, definitely, impossible). "may, might, could" express uncertainty (I'm not sure, not certain, probable, likely, possible, I don't know, perhaps, maybe, I don't think). Sometimes evidence is logical: "Omar has a good memory. He can't have forgotten."

Modal verbs of deduction in the present: لعمل استنتاج قائم على دليل قوي في المضارع نستخدم: Active (معلوم): Subj. + must / can't + inf. Passive (مجهول): Obj. + must / can't + be + p.p.

Examples: Mohammed Salah is healthy and fit. He must care about his health and fitness. He must do a lot of exercises. He can't eat much food. (معلوم) Mohammed Salah is healthy and fit. His health and fitness must be cared about. A lot of exercises must be done by him. Much food can't be eaten by him. (مجهول)

لعمل استنتاج قائم على دليل ضعيف في المضارع نستخدم: Active (معلوم): Subj. + might / may / could + inf. Passive (مجهول): Obj. + might / may / could + be + p.p.

Examples: I've no idea when the new equipment will be delivered. They may / might / could deliver it tomorrow. (معلوم) I've no idea when the new equipment will be delivered. It (may / might / could) be delivered tomorrow. (مجهول)

لعمل استنتاج في المضارع المستمر نستخدم: Subj. + must / can't / might / may / could + be + (inf. + ing)...

Examples: Ayman hasn't answered my call. He must be interviewing some applicants. Reem hasn't told me why she doesn't go out these days. She might be studying for the final exams.

Modal verbs of deduction in the past: لعمل استنتاج قائم على دليل قوي في الماضي نستخدم: Active (معلوم): Subj. + must / can't / couldn't + have + p.p. Passive (مجهول): Obj. + must / can't / couldn't + have + been + p.p.

Examples: Zidan was healthy and fit. He must have cared about his health and fitness. He must have done a lot of exercises. He can't have eaten much food. Zidan was healthy and fit. His health and fitness must have been cared about. A lot of exercises must have been done by him. Much food can't have been eaten by him.

لعمل استنتاج قائم على دليل ضعيف في الماضي نستخدم: Active (معلوم): Subj. + might / may / could + have + p.p. Passive (مجهول): Obj. + might / may / could + have + been + p.p.

Examples: Nadia may / might / could have prepared a big lunch. I'm not sure. A big lunch may / might / could have been prepared by Nadia. I'm not sure.

لعمل استنتاج عن حدث كان مستمراً في الماضي نستخدم: Subj. + must / can't / might / may / could + have + been + (inf. + ing)...

Example: I'm not sure what made Yara not attend my birthday. She might have been helping her mother at home.

Note on "could have": يمكن أن تعبر could have + p.p. عن استنتاج ضعيف أو عن شيء كان من الممكن حدوثه ولكنه لم يحدث: He hasn't visited us for a long time. He could have been busy. (استنتاج ضعيف) He had the ability to fix the computer but he didn't. He could have fixed it. (كان يستطيع ولم يفعل)

Should for advice, blame, or regret: تستخدم (should) لإعطاء النصيحة: Subj. + should / shouldn't + inf. Subj. + ought to / ought not to + inf.

Example: You should work hard to achieve your goal. You shouldn't waste your time.

تستخدم (should + have + p.p.) للتعبير عن اللوم أو الندم: For blame or regret (كان يجب أن يفعل): Subj. + should + have + p.p.

Example: I should have taken my medicine on time. (ندم - كان يجب أن أفعل ذلك)

For blame or regret (كان من المفترض عدم القيام بذلك): Subj. + shouldn't + have + p.p.

Example: You shouldn't have added too much salt. (لوم - كان من المفترض عدم القيام بذلك)

metadata.lesson_title: Global Climate / How to Help Our Environment

text:
Adaptation vs. Modification: adaptation (n) التكيف (التغير للتأقلم مع ظروف جديدة) Example: This film is an adaptation of a famous novel. modification (n) تعديل: (تغيير بسيط لتحسين شيء أو تعديله) Example: We made a modification to the schedule.

Announcement vs. Advertisement: announcement (n) إعلان رسمي أو خبر مهم Example: The PM made an announcement that next Thursday is a holiday. advertisement (n) إعلان تجاري للترويج لمنتج أو خدمة Example: I saw an advertisement for the new phone.

Increase + by, to, in: increase by (يزداد بنسبة) Example: Sales increased by 10% last year. increase to (يزداد إلى حد معين) Example: The price increased to 50 pounds. increase in (يزداد في) Example: Sales increased in numbers and quantity.

Inundation vs. Flooding: inundation (n) فيضان - مصطلح رسمي Example: Inundation caused serious damage to crops. flooding (n) غمر الأرض بالماء Example: Heavy rain led to flooding in the town.

Migration vs. Displacement: migration (n) الهجرة - غالبا طوعية أو دورية Example: Birds begin migration in autumn. displacement (n) النزوح - إجباري Example: The war caused displacement of thousands of people.

Night vs. Overnight: night (n) الليل Example: I slept early last night. overnight (adj/adv) خلال الليل أو بشكل مفاجئ Example: He became famous overnight.

Phenomenon vs. Phenomena: phenomenon (n) ظاهرة Example: A rainbow is a beautiful phenomenon. phenomena (plural) ظواهر Example: Natural phenomena can be unpredictable.

Rather than vs. Other than: rather than (prep/conj) بدلاً من Example: He chose water rather than juice. other than (prep) ما عدا / بخلاف Example: I don't watch TV other than football matches.

metadata.lesson_title: Global Climate / How to Help Our Environment

text: Scientific data indicate that global sea level in 2016 was approximately 82 mm higher than the 1983 average. Projections suggest a potential rise of 0.2 to 2 meters by 2100. This trend is accompanied by a documented increase in the frequency of coastal flooding in numerous regions compared to mid-20th-century levels.

The phenomenon is primarily driven by two connected mechanisms: The accelerated melting of glaciers and ice sheets due to rising global temperatures, which has become the dominant contributor to sea level rise over the past decade. Thermal expansion of seawater as ocean temperatures increase. These processes are expected to intensify under current climate change scenarios.

The implications are severe, with an estimated 275 million people currently residing in low-lying coastal zones exposed to floods. Notably, the majority of the world's megacities are situated in coastal areas, including several major European urban centers facing significant flood risks. Climate migration models predict large population shift in coming decades.

While international climate agreements aim to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions, their potential to slow sea level rise remains uncertain. Consequently, adaptation strategies are becoming increasingly critical. Innovative engineering solutions, such as amphibious architecture and large-scale coastal defense systems, are being implemented in flood-prone regions. These measures demonstrate the growing recognition that both modification and adaptation will be essential to address this pressing global challenge.

When we hear about worldwide issues like climate change, poverty, or hunger, it might seem impossible for one person to make a difference. However, these problems can't be ignored—they must require collective action. If everyone takes small but meaningful steps, the combined impact could be huge.

We should use the knowledge we gain to reflect on our global responsibilities. For instance, reducing waste, supporting ethical businesses, or advocating for fair policies may seem minor, but together, they must create significant change.

The belief that individual actions don't matter couldn't be further from the truth history shows that social shifts often begin with small, insistent efforts. Environmental ruin can't be reversed overnight, but sustainable choices might slow its progression.

Rather than feeling powerless, we should recognize that every positive action contributes to a larger solution. The world's problems must be addressed collectively—and that starts with individual commitment. Change may not happen immediately, but persistence could lead to a fairer, healthier planet for all.

What is a persuasive writing? A persuasive essay is a type of writing that aims to convince the reader to accept a particular opinion or to take a specific action. (المقال الإقناعي هو نوع من الكتابة يهدف إلى إقناع القارئ برأي معين أو دفعه إلى اتخاذ إجراء محدد.)

Purpose of persuasive writing: 1. Convincing the reader to take action (إقناع القارئ ان يتخذ قرار) 2. Changing someone's opinion (تغيير رأي شخص ما) 3. Motivating action (تحفيز اتخاذ إجراء) 4. Presenting a strong argument (عرض حجة قوية)

Key characteristics of Persuasive Writing: 1. Clear Opinion or Point of View: The writer states their opinion or position clearly. (يعبر الكاتب عن وجهة نظره بوضوح.) 2. Strong Reasons and Evidence: The writer gives reasons, facts, examples, or statistics to support their opinion. (يعطي الكاتب اسباب، حقائق، أمثلة أو احصاءات لدعم رأيه.) 3. Emotional Appeal: Uses words that make the reader feel something like fear, hope, guilt, or pride. (يستخدم كلمات تجعل القارئ يشعر بشئ ما مثل الخوف الأمل، الذنب او الفخر.) 4. Persuasive Language: Includes words like: must, should, need to, important, urgent, we all know, clearly, obviously. (تتضمن كلمات قوية مثل: يجب من الضروري من المهم من الواضح.) Uses rhetorical questions: ex. - What will happen if we do nothing? (تستخدم أسئلة بلاغية - ماذا سيحدث اذا لم نفعل شئ ؟) 5. Call to Action: Tells the reader what they should do after reading. (أخبر القارئ بما سيفعل بعد القراءة.)

Persuasive writing terminology: call to action (دعوة لاتخاذ إجراء): A sentence that urges the reader to act. (تحفيز مباشر للقارئ للقيام بشيء.) emotional appeal (مؤثر عاطفي): A sentence that stirs emotions. (جمل تثير المشاعر.) evidence (أدلة): Facts or data supporting your opinion. (أدلة تدعم الرأي.) hook = attention grabber (جملة جذب الانتباه): The first sentence to catch the reader's attention. (الجملة الافتتاحية الجذابة.) opinion (راي): A personal belief or view. (وجهة نظر شخصية.) rhetorical question (سؤال بلاغي): A question asked to provoke thought, not an answer. (سؤال لإثارة التفكير فقط.) thesis statement (جملة الأطروحة): The central idea or argument of the essay. (الفكرة الأساسية التي يدافع عنها الكاتب.) topic sentence (جملة الموضوع): The first sentence in each body paragraph that presents the main idea. (جملة تشرح فكرة الفقرة.)

Summary of Climate Justice and Global Activism: Climate change is not just an environmental issue but also a crisis of social justice. Industrialized nations are the primary cause of greenhouse gas emissions, but marginalized communities, especially in the Global South, bear the brunt of the consequences like droughts and floods. Climate justice advocates for fair solutions that address these unequal burdens. Youth are leading the way in climate activism, creating innovative projects and demanding global action to ensure a fair and sustainable future for all.

Summary of Interview with a Meteorologist: The text is an interview with meteorologist Tamer Mansour about the weather. He explains that meteorologists collect and analyze data to predict the weather, typically for the next five to seven days. He confirms that the recent scorching heatwave in Egypt is unusual and part of a global trend of more frequent extreme weather. While he is somewhat pessimistic about the immediate future, he remains hopeful that long-term solutions to climate change can be found.

Summary of Climate Change and Coastal Cities: Coastal cities like Alexandria are at high risk from climate change due to rising sea levels caused by melting glaciers and ice sheets. Even a small rise in sea level can cause severe flooding in low-lying areas, threatening homes and neighborhoods. Families are worried but also hopeful, as they take precautions and the state works to find solutions. Authorities are investing in renewable energy, building sea walls to protect neighborhoods, and running awareness campaigns to teach citizens how to act during storms.

Summary of The Threat of Rising Sea Levels: Scientific data shows a significant increase in global sea levels, which were approximately 82 mm higher in 2016 than in 1983. Projections indicate a potential sea-level rise of 0.2 to 2 meters by 2100. The primary causes are the accelerated melting of glaciers and the thermal expansion of seawater as it warms. This phenomenon threatens low-lying coastal zones and major cities worldwide, requiring innovative and large-scale solutions to protect communities.

Summary of The Power of Collective Action: Global issues like climate change and poverty can feel overwhelming for a single person. However, individual actions, when combined into a collective effort, can lead to significant and meaningful change. The belief that individual actions don't matter is incorrect; social history shows that change often starts with small, persistent efforts. Sustainable choices and collective persistence are keys to creating a fairer, healthier planet for everyone.

Reading: Read articles and texts about marine ecosystems, including coral reefs and various ocean creatures. Identify key details, define vocabulary from context, and understand idioms and figurative language.
Language: Use and identify different types of adjectives, including participles as adjectives and compound adjectives.
Listening: Extract key information from a podcast about whales and their conservation.
Writing: Write a short story following a specific three-part structure.
Speaking: Discuss opinions about marine life and environmental conservation. Express complaints and how to address them persuasively.

text:
alive → living, not dead. Usually used after a verb like be. تستخدم لوصف كائن أو إنسان ما زال على قيد الحياة ولا تأتي قبل الاسم.
live → happening right now; also used before a noun to mean living. تستخدم لوصف حدث يجرى الآن (بث مباشر) أو قبل اسم للدلالة على أن الشيء حي.
lively → full of energy and activity. مفعم بالحيوية؛ تستخدم لوصف شخص أو مكان مليء بالنشاط والحركة.
breathtaking → extremely beautiful or amazing, making you feel surprised or in awe - خاطف للأنفاس: يستخدم لوصف منظر أو تجربة رائعة جدا تجعل الشخص مندهشا.
breath less → out of breath - unable to breathe easily, often because of excitement, fear, or physical effort - لاهث: يُستخدم لوصف حالة انقطاع النفس أو صعوبة التنفس بسبب مجهود أو شعور قوي.
colored → having a particular color or painted in a certain color - مصبوغ / ملون: يُشير إلى وجود لون معين على الشيء عن طريق التلوين أو الصباغة.
colorful → having many bright and different colors; also used to describe something interesting or exciting - ملون / زاه: يُستخدم لوصف أشياء ذات ألوان كثيرة ومشرقة، أو تصف شيئًا شيقا أو مثيرا.
fragile → easily broken or damaged; delicate - هش: يستخدم لوصف شيء يمكن أن يتلف بسهولة.
weak → not physically strong; lacking power or ability - ضعيف: يستخدم لوصف قلة القوة الجسدية أو المعنوية.
haunting → staying in your mind because it is beautiful or sad - مؤثر: يُستخدم لوصف ذكرى أو لحن أو منظر يبقى في الذاكرة.
hunted → chased or looked for by someone, often to be captured or killed - مطارد: يستخدم لوصف شخص أو حيوان يتم ملاحقته.
known for → famous because of a particular quality or achievement - معروف بـ: يُستخدم عند ذكر سبب شهرة الشخص أو الشيء.
known as → having a particular name or role - معروف كـ يُستخدم لتحديد الاسم أو الوظيفة.
known to → familiar to someone - معروف لدي / عند : يُستخدم للتعبير عن شيء مألوف لشخص ما.
pose → to create or present (a problem, danger, or question); also to position oneself for a photo - يُشكل / يطرح: يُستخدم لوصف خطر أو تحد أو سؤال، أو عند الوقوف لالتقاط صورة.
pause → to stop doing something for a short time - يَتَوَفَّف للحظة: للتوقف المؤقت قبل الاستمرار.
vivid → very clear, bright, or detailed - زاه: يستخدم لوصف لون أو صورة أو وصف دقيق وواضح.
vibrant → full of life, energy, and strong colours - مفعم بالحيوية: يستخدم لوصف مكان أو شخصية مليئة بالنشاط أو الألوان القوية.

metadata.lesson_title: A Colorful Underwater City / The Ocean's Gentle Giants

text: Beneath the surface of the warm, crystal-clear ocean lies a breathtaking world known as the coral reef. Often called a colorful underwater city, the reef is home to thousands of marine species, from graceful sea turtles to tiny, brightly-colored fish. These ecosystems are incredibly diverse and provide essential shelter, food, and protection for ocean life. Coral reefs grow slowly over thousands of years, but they are surprisingly fragile. Pollution, climate change, and destructive fishing practices pose serious threats to these natural wonders. When conditions are poor, corals bleach, losing their vivid colors, and becoming lifeless. In contrast, a healthy reef is alive with motion and brilliant colors. Marine biologists work tirelessly to protect coral reefs. Their efforts include educating communities and promoting eco-friendly tourism. As the saying goes, a stitch in time saves nine - acting early can prevent long-term damage. We must act now, before the reefs become a thing of the past. Some people may underestimate the importance of coral reefs, but they are priceless. They not only support marine life, but also protect coastal areas from storms and erosion. With renewed effort and widespread awareness, we can help these underwater cities recover and thrive again. Every little action makes a difference - because in the ocean, everything is connected.

Whales are some of the ocean's most grand creatures, yet today, many species face critical threats. These gentle giants, known for their haunting songs and massive size, are struggling against human-made dangers. Noisy ship traffic disrupts their communication, while deadly fishing nets trap them accidentally. Pollution makes their migratory journeys even more hazardous. But there's hope! Dedicated scientists are working tirelessly to protect them. Using non-invasive drones, they track whale movements without disturbance. Stricter laws now ban harmful sonar in protected areas, and conservation groups rescue entangled whales. Even small actions - like reducing plastic waste - help preserve their fragile habitats. Why does this matter? Whales play a vital role in our ecosystem. Their survival ensures healthier oceans for all. So next time you see a news story about these magnificent animals, remember: their future depends on us.

The underwater world is full of beauty and wonders. It's a fascinating and little-known place. Beneath the surface of the deep-blue oceans, there are brightly-colored corals, strange-looking plants, and thousands of amazing sea creatures. Scientists emphasize that the ocean is full of life, even more than the land we live on. Every deep-sea journey shows us new wonders from this hidden world. In warm tropical seas, brightly-colored fish swim through coral reefs, while slow-moving turtles glide gracefully through the water. Dolphins use high-pitched sounds to talk to one another, and whales sing long-distance songs that can travel for miles. Deeper in the ocean, the water becomes ice-cold and really-dark. Unusual creatures survive there, some with light-producing bodies that help them find food. Sadly, the underwater world faces many human-made problems. Plastic-filled waters, overfishing, and fast-rising temperatures damage marine life. Coral reefs are dying, and sea animals are losing their safe spaces. To protect this fragile but beautiful environment, countries build protected marine areas, scientists study endangered species, and people are encouraged to use eco-friendly habits like recycling. Working together, we can keep the underwater world safe for future generations.

metadata.lesson_title: Into the Depth of the Ocean

text: The ocean is home to a wide variety of amazing creatures. Some are deep-sea animals that live in complete darkness, using glow-producing organs to attract prey. Others, like dolphins, are intelligent and social, often seen diving through the waves in coordinated groups. Sharks have an unbelievable sense of smell and can detect a single drop of blood from miles away. Despite their reputation, most sharks are not dangerous to humans. In fact, many species are endangered because of overfishing and pollution. Coral reefs are another awe-inspiring part of marine life. These colorful underwater ecosystems are built by tiny animals called coral polyps. They support thousands of marine species and are often referred to as the rainforests of the sea. Sadly, rising sea temperatures and human activities have caused coral bleaching, putting these habitats at risk. Sea turtles are known for their long-distance migrations and their strong connection to the beach where they were born. After hatching, baby turtles face a hard-to-survive journey to the sea. Many do not make it. Marine biologists work hard to protect the ocean. They study fast-changing environments and create awareness about conservation. Protecting marine life is not just important - it's life-saving. It's clear that we cannot afford to underestimate the value of the ocean. If we turn a blind eye to the damage being done, we might find ourselves in deep water literally and figuratively.

metadata.lesson_title: Into the Depth of the Ocean

text: Many years ago, in a quiet fishing village, people spoke of a strange legend. They believed a sea woman lived in the deepest part of the ocean. Her voice was so heart-stoppingly beautiful that it could calm storms or cause them. One summer evening, young Elías was out fishing alone. The sea was calm, but suddenly, dark clouds gathered. Thunder crashed, and waves crashed against his boat. Then, through the roaring wind, he heard singing. It was soft, sweet, and haunting. He followed the sound. There, sitting on a rock in the stormy sea, was a glowing figure with long silver hair. Elias stared, unable to move. Go home, the sea woman whispered. The sea is angry tonight. Elias turned his boat around. As he sailed away, the storm slowly faded. By the time he reached the shore, the sky was clear again. The next day, no one believed his story. But Elias never forgot the voice. He became a wise sailor, always respectful of the ocean. They say if you listen carefully on stormy nights, you can still hear the sea woman's song - reminding people that the sea is beautiful, powerful and full of secrets.

text: It is an adjective that has two or more words. هي صفة تتكون من كلمتين أو أكثر. Example: Mohammed Salah is a well-known footballer. Omar is a never-to-be-forgotten leader.

Compound Adjectives with Numbers: Formation: Number - Singular Noun - Adjective. Example: My son is a twelve-year-old child. My house is in a ten-metre-wide street. Formation: Number - Singular Noun. Example: Mr Omar is an expert on twentieth-century history. We're going on a ten-day trip. It's a four-hour journey. Formation: Number - Plural Noun ending in s or s'. Used for distance, travel, and time expressions (drive, walk, swim, fly, ride). Do not use a/an before these. Example: My work is two hours' drive from here. We have three hours' walk every weekend. Time Expressions: She'll be back in a week's time. In two weeks' time, I am traveling to Spain.

Compound Adjectives Starting with Adjectives: Adj. + P.P.: absent-minded (شارد الذهن / كثير النسيان), big-eared (كبير الأذنين), clear-cut (واضح / محدد), deep-seated (متأصل), green-eyed (أخضر العينين), high-spirited (ذو معنويات مرتفعة), kind-hearted (طيب القلب), long-awaited (طال انتظاره), long-haired (طويل الشعر), long-nosed (طويل الأنف), long-sighted (لديه طول نظر), middle-aged (في منتصف العمر), narrow-minded (ضيق الأفق), old-fashioned (قديم), open-minded (واسع الأفق), quick-witted (سريع البديهة), red-handed (مُتلبس / مذنب), short-haired (قصير الشعر), short-sighted (لديه قصر نظر), strong-willed (قوي الإرادة), tight-fisted (بخيل). Adj. + -ing: good-looking (جذاب), slow-moving (بطيء الحركة), long-lasting (يدوم طويلاً), far-reaching (بعيد المدى), easy-going (لين / هادئ الطباع). Adj. + Noun: last-minute (في اللحظة الأخيرة), full-length (بالطول الكامل), second-hand (مستعمل).

Compound Adjectives Starting with Adverbs: Adv. + P.P.: brightly lit (ساطع الإضاءة), badly written (مكتوب بشكل سيء), highly respected (يحظى باحترام كبير), densely populated (كثيف السكان), well-educated (متعلم جيداً), well-known (معروف / مشهور), well-balanced (متزن), widely recognized (معروف على نطاق واسع), well-behaved (حسن السلوك), well-paid (ذو أجر مرتفع), widely spoken (يتم التحدث بها على نطاق واسع), well-informed (مطلع). Adv. + -ing: never-ending (دائم / لا ينتهي), forward-thinking (سابق لعصره), ever-lasting (دائم).

Compound Adjectives Starting with Nouns: Noun + P.P.: sun-baked (مجفف بالشمس), child-wanted (محبوب من الأطفال), middle-aged (في منتصف العمر), left-handed (أعسر), right-handed (يستخدم اليد اليمنى), heart-broken (حزين / منفطر القلب). Noun + -ing: record-breaking (محطم الرقم القياسي), mouth-watering (شهي / لذيذ), time-saving (موفر للوقت), thought-provoking (مثير للتفكير), labour-saving (موفر للعمالة), English-speaking (متحدث بالإنجليزية). Noun + Adj.: ice-cold (في برودة الثلج), brand-new (جديد تماماً), world-famous (عالمي الشهرة), self-reliant (معتمد على نفسه), fat-free (خالي من الدهون), self-centered (أناني), smoke-free (ممنوع التدخين). Noun + Noun: egg-box (صندوق بيض), tea-pot (براد شاي), hair-cream (كريم شعر), eye-drop (قطرة عين), part-time (جزئي).

Use a hyphen (-) between parts of a compound adjective only if it comes before the noun. If the noun does not follow, do not use a hyphen. Example: Mohammed Salah is a well-known footballer. As a footballer, Mohammed Salah is well known. Never use a hyphen between the adverb very and the adjective it modifies. Example: Ahmed is a very clever student. Comparison and Superlative of Compound Adjectives: good-looking becomes better-looking / more good-looking or best-looking / most good-looking. well-known becomes better-known / more well-known or best-known / most well-known.
A short story is a brief work of fiction that focuses on a single event, character, or theme. It is designed to be read in one sitting and often leaves a strong impression on the reader.

metadata.lesson_title: Into the Depth of the Ocean

text: Main Parts of a Short Story: 1. Beginning (Introduction): Introduces characters, setting, and sometimes hints at the conflict. 2. Middle (Development): Shows the main events, builds tension, and develops the conflict. 3. End (Resolution): Resolves the conflict and shows the outcome. Detailed Techniques of Short-Story Writing: theme: the main message or moral lesson. characters: believable people/animals with clear traits. setting: where and when the story happens. plot: the sequence of events (Beginning-Middle-End). conflict: the main problem or challenge. tension & suspense: feelings that keep readers engaged. climax: the most exciting turning point. resolution: how the problem is solved. dialogue: conversations that reveal character and advance the plot. style & tone: the writer's way of expression and emotional attitude.

metadata.lesson_title: Into the Depth of the Ocean

text: One quiet morning, Mrs Nevine, Rodayna's Arabic teacher, stood before the class with a mysterious smile. Today, she announced, We will have a surprise quiz. Rodayna's heart sank. By the end of the lesson, the papers were collected, and the truth was undeniable - she failed miserably. A flush of embarrassment burned her cheeks, and disappointment weighed on her like a heavy cloak. That evening, her father, Mr Mohammed, found her sitting silently at her desk. He placed a gentle hand on her shoulder. You have a sharp mind, Rodayna, he said in a warm but firm voice, but you are letting time slip through your fingers. Time, once gone, never returns. If you learn to guide it, you can guide your life. His words struck something deep within her. That very night, she sketched a new plan for herself: work first, leisure later. It was not easy. Messages from friends buzzed on her phone, and the pull of distraction was strong. Yet, step by step, she held her course. Two months later, her grades rose, her mind was lighter, and her heart was proud. She learned her lesson well-time was a treasure, and she would guard it fiercely.

Reading: Read articles and stories about the changing workplace, the impact of AI, and the importance of human skills. Identify key details, define idioms, and answer comprehension questions.
Language: Use causative verbs (have and get) in various tenses to talk about having someone else perform a task or having a task done by someone.
Listening: Extract key information from an audio text about the skills needed for the future workforce.
Writing: Write a narrative story about a day in the future, using descriptive language and a clear plot structure.
Speaking: Express personal opinions through debates and discussions about the pros and cons of AI in the workplace, and the importance of human connection and "soft skills".
